fix(dynamic-vars): anchor regex validation to match PAPI fullmatch semantics

JS `RegExp.test()` is a partial match — `[0-9]+` passes on `"123abc"`.
PAPI uses `re.fullmatch` (anchored). Wrapping patterns in `^(?:...)$`
makes UI validation consistent with backend rejection, preventing false
\"valid\" indicators before a 400 at run-request creation.

Applies to RunTimeVarFields, RunTimeVarOverrideDialog, and the default-
value check in CodeAndSettingsWidget.

perf(fork): O(1) isLastItemInTurn via pre-computed map

Replace timeline.slice(i+1).some(...) in both message-response and
tool-response render branches with a single O(N) forward pass that
records the last timeline position for each invocationId. Each render
iteration now does a Map.get() lookup instead of slicing and scanning
the tail — O(N²) → O(N) overall for long sessions.

Rw 779 slackfix (#3518)

* centralise Slack ownership in slackbot service (RW-779)

Architectural follow-up to RW-779. PAPI's task-worker no longer reads
Vault, calls slack_sdk, or enforces hooks.slack.com host allow-lists
for the scheduled-chat dispatch path. All Slack interaction now lives

PAPI-side path; this seam goes away entirely.

- slackbot/routers/workflow.py:

    callers are unaffected.

RW-779 — Scheduled workspace chat sessions (PAPI + UI) (#3511)

* feat(scheduling): PAPI scheduler + dispatcher + Studio UI for scheduled
chat (RW-779)

Wires the PAPI side of RW-779 (the agentfarm side committed separately)
plus the Studio UI for configuring, previewing, and tracking scheduled
chat commands.

Backend (PAPI / FastAPI):

* papi/tasks/scheduled_chat_tasks.py — three-tier taskiq fan-out for
  per-workspace failure isolation. Tier 1 heartbeats every minute and
  fans out one kiq per workspace; tier 2 evaluates due commands per
  workspace; tier 3 fires one command end-to-end. retry_on_error=False
  on tiers 2/3 so a flaky downstream doesn't desync last_fired_at and
  pile up duplicate sessions.
* papi/services/scheduled_chat_dispatch.py — sink-aware dispatcher.
  email mode=user via Mailgun + Jinja template
  (scheduled_chat_artifact.html.jinja). email mode=all-workspace-users
  resolves the workspace member list at FIRE TIME (not configure time)
  so additions/removals take effect on the very ne

address Rohit MEDIUM follow-ups (RW-779)

MED-1 — Slack install error containment
  _build_slack_dispatch_context now wraps _resolve_slack_install in
  try/except. A DB hiccup during install lookup previously propagated
  up and aborted the entire dispatch (failing unrelated email +
  webhook sinks); it now surfaces a sanitized error against channel
  sinks only and lets the rest of asyncio.gather run.

MED-2 — slack_channel_workspaces upsert idempotency
  upsert_channel_for_team replaces delete-then-insert with
  select-then-update-or-insert. The old approach raced with concurrent
  member_joined_channel events: T1 and T2 both saw the row, both
  deleted, both inserted -> 2 rows for the same (slack_workspace_id,
  slack_channel) pair. The new approach updates in place when the row
  exists, so sequential re-captures (the realistic case) never
  duplicate. The truly-concurrent first-time race is unchanged but
  rare; a unique index on (slack_workspace_id, slack_channel) WHERE
  slack_channel IS NOT NULL is th

fix(papi): read slack bot token without slackbot import (RW-779)

Test deploy logs showed every slack-channel dispatch failing with:

  ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'slackbot'
  File scheduled_chat_dispatch.py, line 334, in _read_slack_bot_token_sync
    from slackbot.slack_install import Vault

The dispatcher runs in the task-worker container, which doesn't ship
the slackbot/ package — each FASTAPI_SERVICE has its own image. The
slackbot.Vault helper is only a thin hvac wrapper around
/v1/org/data/none/system/slackbot/{enterprise_id}-{team_id}, so we
read that secret directly via utils.vaultutils.get_vault_client +
hvac KV2 instead of going through slackbot.

Email + slack-webhook delivery were unaffected (neither path needed
the bot token); only mode=channel was broken.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
Merge branch 'main' into rw-779

address Rohit review comments on RW-779

- chat_config dry-run: release the DB session back to the pool before
  the long agentfarm HTTP call (up to 600s). Holding it across the
  round-trip lets a handful of concurrent admin dry-runs starve regular
  request traffic at pool size=20+overflow=10.

- chat_config dry-run: add a per-workspace fixed-window rate limit
  (4 dry-runs / 60s, Redis-backed). Each dry-run is a full LLM session
  in agentfarm; throttle prevents an admin iterating in the schedule UI
  from saturating worker capacity. Fails open on Redis errors since the
  endpoint is already admin-gated.

- mint_run_as_token: enforce a READ_AND_RUN minimum role for the workspace
  permission row. Matches the gate #3505 added to run-session endpoints —
  without it, a workspace admin could schedule a session to run as a
  READ_ONLY member and the minted JWT would be accepted by PAPI auth.
  New unit tests cover ADMIN/READ_AND_RUN accept paths and READ_ONLY
  rejection.

- _close_slack_client: callable() gua

feat(chat): in-session feedback-prompt dialog (UI) — RW-768 (#3502)

* feat(chat): in-session feedback-prompt dialog (UI) — RW-768

Render a thumbs-up / thumbs-down / cancel dialog in place of the chat
composer when agentfarm emits a feedbackPromptRequested SSE event.
Up/down reuse the existing submitSessionFeedback endpoint; cancel just
dismisses. All three close the dialog and restore the input. The prompt
never renders in read-only (chat export) mode, and the session store
dedupes on feedbackPromptTriggerKey so an SSE retry can't re-open a
dismissed prompt in the same turn.

Paired server change: runwhen/agentfarm#187.

were admin-only, so non-admin members landing on the chat config page

  canAdmin defaults to false (fail-closed) on the dialog props.

fix(sobow-index): prevent event loop starvation during codecollection indexing (#3459)

* fix(sobow-index): prevent event loop starvation during codecollection indexing

CPU-bound work in the codecollection indexing pipeline was blocking the
async event loop for extended periods, causing health/liveness probes to
time out and ASGI transport errors when the operator's connection closed.

Three targeted fixes:

1. _upsert: offload JSON serialization of large vector payloads (500
   points × ~768 floats ≈ 3-4 MB per batch) to a thread via
   asyncio.to_thread, freeing the event loop during the heaviest
   single-operation blocker.

2. _do_rebuild: yield to the event loop every 50 entries in the
   embedding generation loop (3000+ sequential iterations) so health
   probe handlers can be served between embeddings.

3. upsert_knowledgebase: yield between batch upserts so the event loop
   isn't blocked across consecutive heavy I/O + serialization cycles.

causes isLoggedIn to become true, triggering the render-body redirect
to '/' which competes with push('/success'). The two competing
router.push calls cancel each other, leaving the user stuck on the
login page. Guard the redirect with !isSubmitting so it only fires
for the "already logged in" case, not during active login submission.

* fix: initialize prevCommandsRef as null so cached data triggers card render (RW-303)

chore(assistant-searches): reduce calls to sobow & increase response size (#2560)

**Changes:**

1. Added Dynamic Search Results Limit: Added "topK": 25 + 4 * len(query)
parameter to _search_workspace_tasks to scale the number of returned
results based on the number of queries.

2. Query Deduplication & Batching: Refactored _resolve_issues_to_tasks
to collect and deduplicate queries across all issues before making a
single search call, replacing the previous approach of making separate
calls for each issue.

3. Improved Result Mapping: Added new logic to map search results back
to their originating issues using a query_to_issues dictionary, while
maintaining the same output structure for backward compatibility. Each
issue's results now include a list of queries and their corresponding
responses.

The main goal appears to be optimizing performance by reducing API calls
while maintaining the same interface and functionality.
